title: Lesson 4: Serving as Mission
description: Praying, listening, and eating have built the bridge. Now it is time to be inconvenienced for someone else's good. Jesus came not to be served but to serve, and He calls His followers into the same posture. This lesson shows you how to serve from freedom rather than for approval, with the time, talents, and treasure already in your hand.

blocks.text: In Lesson 1 you saw that you are sent. Lesson 2 gave you the BLESS rhythms. Lesson 3 turned your home and table into a place of welcome. Now you arrive at the most active rhythm of the five. Serving is where you stop being only present and start being inconvenienced for someone else. It is where the bridge of relationship gets weight to walk on. And before any of it begins, Jesus has something to show you. Not a sermon, a scene.

blocks.alt: Jesus kneeling with a basin and a towel, washing the feet of His disciples at the Last Supper

blocks.title: The Towel and the Basin
blocks.text:
It was the night before the cross. Jesus and His disciples sat down for the Passover meal. Jesus knew exactly what was coming for Him, that Judas was about to betray Him, that Peter was about to deny Him, and that the rest were about to scatter. He had every reason to demand attention, ask for comfort, or talk about His coming death.

Instead, He stood up from the table, took off His outer robe, wrapped a towel around His waist, poured water into a basin, and began to wash His disciples' feet. The Lord became the slave. The teacher took the lowest job in the house.

"Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going back to God, rose from supper. He laid aside his outer garments, and taking a towel, tied it around his waist. Then he poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples' feet." (John 13:3-5)

Peter could not stand it. "You shall never wash my feet," he said. Jesus answered, "If I do not wash you, you have no part with me." Then He sat back down and gave them the line they would never forget. "If I then, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet." (John 13:14)

Jesus had every right to be served. He chose to serve. He had hours left to live, and He spent them washing dirty feet. That is the picture He wanted His followers to remember. Not power, not status. A towel and a basin. Serving in the kingdom of God starts here, with the King Himself on His knees.
Greatness, Upside Down

A few days earlier, the mother of two of those disciples had pulled Jesus aside with a request. She wanted her sons, James and John, to sit at His right hand and left hand when He came into His kingdom. She wanted them at the top. The other ten disciples heard about it and got angry. Jesus called them all together and said something they would never have expected.

You know that the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their great ones exercise authority over them. It shall not be so among you. But whoever would be great among you must be your servant, and whoever would be first among you must be your slave, even as the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

The world has a clear definition of greatness. Climb the ladder, build the brand, get the corner office, have more people serving you than you serve. Jesus had a different definition entirely. He said greatness in His kingdom is measured by who you serve, not by who serves you. The world counts followers, but Jesus counts feet washed. The world says rise up, but Jesus says kneel down. The first will be last and the last will be first, because the way down is the way up in His kingdom.
4.1 - Where in your life are you tempted to define greatness the way the world defines it? Where would Jesus' definition of greatness change how you spend this week?
Why You Can Serve Without Earning

This is where most Christians get stuck. They hear serve others and feel the weight of one more religious requirement, one more thing they have to do to keep God happy. The gospel does something with serving that no other religion does. It frees you to serve without trying to earn anything by it.
For you were called to freedom, brothers. Only do not use your freedom as an opportunity for the flesh, but through love serve one another.

Paul does not say serve God to earn your standing with Him. He says you have already been called to freedom, and the love that flows out of that freedom is what serves your neighbor. Jesus has already paid your debt. Your standing before God is settled. You have nothing left to prove and nothing left to earn. That settled standing is exactly what frees you to serve. You can wash someone's feet without needing them to wash yours back, because your worth has already been paid for in someone else's blood.

Pastor John Piper offers a picture worth holding onto. He says God is not a watering trough you have to keep filling up. God is a mountain spring, always flowing, always overflowing, always supplying. You do not glorify a mountain spring by hauling buckets of water up the path and pouring them in. You glorify a mountain spring by getting down on your knees, drinking, and being filled until you overflow into others. That is what serving is supposed to be. You drink from Jesus, He fills you up, and the world watches the overflow.
Whoever serves, as one who serves by the strength that God supplies, in order that in everything God may be glorified through Jesus Christ. To him belong glory and dominion forever and ever. Amen.

Notice what Peter says. The strength comes from God, the serving is yours, the glory goes back to Him. Serving in your own strength runs dry by Tuesday. Serving by the strength God supplies is the kind that keeps going for a lifetime.
4.2 - Where do you feel pressure to serve like a watering trough, hauling buckets to keep something full? What would change this week if you served from the spring instead?
Time, Talents, and Treasure

Service is the everyday way you pour out three things. Your time, your talents, and your treasure. Time means hours and minutes given to people who will never pay you back. Talents are the skills God built into you, used for someone outside your circle. Treasure is your money, your home, your tools, your stuff. Serving means letting any of those three be inconvenienced for the sake of another person's need. None of it was ever yours to clutch. All of it was given to you to give.

Inside the BLESS rhythms, this is the moment everything you have been doing turns into action. You have been praying for them, listening to them, eating with them. Now you are going to inconvenience yourself for them. You are going to use one of your three Ts to make their life lighter. That is what God's kingdom looks like in your neighborhood. Tangible and specific. A casserole dropped off on a hard night, an afternoon spent helping someone move, a small bill paid quietly with no expectation of anything in return.

4.3 - Of your time, talents, and treasure, which one is hardest for you to give to someone who could not pay you back? What is one specific way you could give it this week to a person you have been BLESSing?
Let Them Serve You First

There is a quiet but important piece of Jesus' approach to mission. When He sent the disciples out into the villages, He told them to find a person of peace and stay in that person's home. Then He told them something most Western Christians skip right past. Eat what is set in front of you. Receive what they give you. The disciples were told to receive before they gave.

Whatever house you enter, first say, 'Peace be to this house!' And if a son of peace is there, your peace will rest upon him. But if not, it will return to you. And remain in the same house, eating and drinking what they provide, for the laborer deserves his wages.

Jesus did not tell them to march in and start fixing things. He told them to receive hospitality first. Eat their food. Sleep in their beds. Let them serve you before you serve them. There is humility, mutuality, and trust being built in that. The world says serving is a one-way ladder where you serve down and never up. Jesus says serving is a two-way street.

Letting your neighbor pour you a cup of coffee, fix your fence, watch your kids, or pray for your family before you ever offer to do the same says something powerful. It says, you are not my project, you are my equal, my friend, my image-bearing neighbor. The trust that builds is what makes deeper service welcome later.
4.4 - When was the last time someone offered to serve you and you said no? What would it look like to say yes this week, and what does saying yes communicate to that person?
The Joy of the Towel

One last truth, because the Christian life cannot be sustained by duty alone. Jesus did not wash the disciples' feet because He felt obligated. Hebrews 12 says He endured the cross for the joy that was set before Him. Joy was what kept Him kneeling. And joy is what God means to put inside you as you serve.

We want you to know, brothers, about the grace of God that has been given among the churches of Macedonia, for in a severe test of affliction, their abundance of joy and their extreme poverty have overflowed in a wealth of generosity on their part.

The poorer the Macedonians got, the more they wanted to give. That is not human nature. That is the Spirit at work. That is the spring overflowing. Pastor John Piper puts it like this: love is the overflow of joy in God that gladly meets the needs of others. Service that begins as obligation will run dry by Tuesday. Service that begins as joy in Jesus is the kind that lasts a lifetime.
This Week's Practice

Pick one person from your BLESS list. Identify one specific way to serve them this week with your time, talent, or treasure. Make it simple: drop off a meal, watch their kids for an hour, help with the yard, or quietly cover a small bill they did not ask you to cover. Do it without expecting anything in return. Then ask the Spirit to fill you up again before the next week. That is the rhythm: drink, overflow, drink again.
Lesson 4 Review

Jesus came not to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many. Greatness in His kingdom is measured by who you wash, not who washes you. You serve from freedom rather than to earn approval. You overflow from a spring rather than haul buckets to a trough. You give your time, talents, and treasure to people who could never pay you back. And you let your neighbor serve you first, because mutuality is what trust is built on. In the next lesson, the bridge gets walked across. The praying, listening, eating, and serving have earned the conversation. Now you have something to say.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
